title: Find Writable Directories Missing the Sticky Bit
hook: A writable log directory is not the same thing as a safe shared directory.
problem: You need to find directories that any user can write to, but where users can also rename or delete each other's files.
starting_state: An app tree contains deploy directories, shared storage, and an overly open log folder.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists world-writable directories that lack sticky-bit protection.
when_to_use: Use during permission audits when shared write paths may have been opened too broadly.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every result is exploitable without checking service users and directory purpose.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Mode, owner, group, and path for writable directories missing the sticky bit.

shorts.title: Find unsafe writable dirs.
shorts.voiceover: World-writable directories deserve a second look. Filter for paths missing the sticky bit so the audit starts with the risky ones.
shorts.caption: Writable is not always safely shared.

linkedin.question: Do you check sticky-bit gaps separately from world-writable paths?
linkedin.cta: Audit shared write paths before changing modes.

youtube.description: Find world-writable directories that do not have sticky-bit protection.

title: Find Release Files Writable Outside the Owner
hook: A release file that someone besides the owner can modify deserves a second look.
problem: You need to spot files under a release directory that are writable by group or other users.
starting_state: A release directory contains config, public files, scripts, and an upload export with broader write bits.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reports files writable outside the owning user.
when_to_use: Use after deploys, restores, packaging changes, or chmod cleanup when release file mutability matters.
when_not_to_use: Do not blindly remove group write from runtime files without checking how the service writes them.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of release files with group-write or other-write bits.

shorts.title: Find writable release files.
shorts.voiceover: When reviewing a release tree, list files writable outside the owner before changing modes. The write bit tells you where to inspect first.
shorts.caption: Writable release files deserve review.

linkedin.question: Do you check release files for group or world write bits before chmod cleanup?
linkedin.cta: Find broad write access before changing permissions.

youtube.description: Find release files that are writable by group or other users.

experiments.variant_a: Find broad write bits in releases.

title: Find Runtime Directories Writable Outside the Owner
hook: Runtime directories often need writes, but the write boundary should be visible.
problem: You need to review storage, cache, log, and upload directories that are writable by group or other users.
starting_state: A release tree has storage, cache, logs, and uploads directories with different write modes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists runtime directories writable outside the owning user.
when_to_use: Use when reviewing writable application paths before hardening or incident cleanup.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat every result as wrong; caches, uploads, and logs may intentionally allow service-group writes.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Runtime directories with group-write or other-write permission bits.

shorts.title: Review writable runtime dirs.
shorts.voiceover: Storage, cache, logs, and uploads may need writes. List the directories writable outside the owner so the boundary is explicit.
shorts.caption: Writable runtime paths need intent.

linkedin.question: Do you separate runtime write paths from static release files during permission audits?
linkedin.cta: Review writable directories before broad chmod fixes.

youtube.description: Find runtime directories that are writable by group or other users.

experiments.variant_b: Find broad writes in runtime paths.

title: Find SUID, SGID, and Sticky Bits in an App Tree
hook: Special bits are easy to miss in a long ls listing.
problem: You need a compact inventory of files and directories with SUID, SGID, or sticky bits set.
starting_state: An application tree contains normal executables plus special-mode files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command inventories special permission bits.
when_to_use: Use during hardening reviews, after vendor installs, or before approving a deployment image.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove special bits until you know whether the program depends on them.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of paths with any SUID, SGID, or sticky bit set.

shorts.title: Audit special bits.
shorts.voiceover: SUID and SGID are not normal execute bits. List every special bit in the app tree before deciding what belongs there.
shorts.caption: Special bits should be intentional.

linkedin.question: How often do you inventory SUID and SGID files outside system paths?

youtube.description: Find SUID, SGID, and sticky-bit paths in an application tree.

experiments.variant_b: Find hidden SUID and SGID files.

title: Group Writable Files by Owning Group
hook: Group-writable files are not automatically wrong, but the owning group decides the risk.
problem: You need to see which groups can modify files under an application tree.
starting_state: Some runtime paths are intentionally group-writable, and others may be accidental.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command groups writable files by their owning group.
when_to_use: Use when service groups, deploy groups, or shared upload directories are under review.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat group-writable as bad by itself; runtime directories often need it.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Group names, modes, and paths for group-writable files.

shorts.title: Audit group writes.
shorts.voiceover: Do not just ask whether a file is writable. Ask which group can write it, then decide whether that group makes sense.
shorts.caption: The group tells you the risk.

linkedin.question: Do you review group-writable files by group name or just by mode?
linkedin.cta: Start the audit with group context.

youtube.description: List group-writable files with their owning group for safer permission triage.

experiments.variant_a: The group tells you the risk.

title: Trace Every Parent Directory on a Permission Denial
hook: The file mode can look fine while a parent directory blocks the whole path.
problem: You need to inspect each directory component in a path to find where traversal permission fails.
starting_state: A symlinked current release points to a config file whose access depends on parent directory modes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command shows each path component with owner and mode.
when_to_use: Use when a process gets permission denied even though the final file looks readable.
when_not_to_use: Do not stop at the final file mode; parent execute bits and symlink targets matter.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A component-by-component ownership and mode trace for the full path.

shorts.title: Trace the whole path.
shorts.voiceover: Permission denied is not always about the file. Namei shows every parent directory and symlink in the path.
shorts.caption: Check the whole path, not just the file.

linkedin.question: When permission denied makes no sense, do you check parent directories first?
linkedin.cta: Trace the path before changing modes.

youtube.description: Use namei to inspect parent directories and symlinks during permission-denied triage.

experiments.variant_b: The parent directory may block access.

title: Find World-Readable Secret-Looking Files
hook: The fastest secret audit starts with readable files that look like secrets.
problem: You need to find files with sensitive names that are readable by everyone.
starting_state: An app tree contains config files, token symlinks, and at least one overly readable secret-looking file.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists suspicious readable files for review.
when_to_use: Use during production handoff, incident triage, or before publishing an artifact.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat filename matching as a complete secrets scan; it is a fast first pass.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: World-readable files whose names suggest secrets, keys, tokens, or environment config.

shorts.title: Find readable secrets.
shorts.voiceover: Start secret exposure triage with files that look sensitive and are readable by everyone.
shorts.caption: Readable secrets are the first pass.

linkedin.question: What filename patterns do you include in a fast secret-permission audit?
linkedin.cta: Triage readable secrets before broader scanning.

youtube.description: Find secret-looking files that are readable by all users.

experiments.variant_a: Readable secrets are the first pass.
experiments.variant_b: Find secrets exposed by mode bits.

title: Find Config Files with Execute Bits
hook: Config files do not usually need to be executable.
problem: You need to find non-binary configuration files that accidentally have execute permissions.
starting_state: A config directory contains a normal env file, a secret key, and an executable config file.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists config-like files with any execute bit set.
when_to_use: Use when auditing packaged releases, copied configs, or files created from templates.
when_not_to_use: Do not apply this to directories or legitimate executable scripts.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Config-like files that have owner, group, or other execute bits.

shorts.title: Find executable configs.
shorts.voiceover: Execute bits on config files are usually drift. Find them quickly before you start changing modes.
shorts.caption: Config files rarely need execute bits.

linkedin.question: Do you flag executable config files in release audits?
linkedin.cta: List suspicious execute bits before cleanup.

youtube.description: Find config-like files that accidentally have execute permissions.

experiments.variant_a: Config files rarely need execute bits.

title: Audit a Symlink Permission Chain
hook: A symlink can make the path you audited different from the file the app opens.
problem: You need to inspect symlink targets and every parent directory before deciding whether permissions are wrong.
starting_state: A current release symlink and a config symlink point across the app tree.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists symlinks and traces their resolved path components.
when_to_use: Use when current-release, shared-secret, or uploads symlinks complicate a permission issue.
when_not_to_use: Do not audit only the symlink inode; access depends on the target path too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Each symlink target followed by namei ownership and mode details.

shorts.title: Trace symlink permissions.
shorts.voiceover: When a path includes symlinks, audit the target chain. The file you see is not always the file the service opens.
shorts.caption: Symlinks change the permission story.

linkedin.question: Do release symlinks make your permission audits harder?
linkedin.cta: Trace symlink targets before changing ownership.

youtube.description: Use find and namei to audit symlink targets and their permission chain.

experiments.variant_b: Audit the target, not just the link.

title: Find Upload Files Writable Outside the Owner
hook: Uploads are supposed to be writable at the edge, not writable forever by everyone.
problem: You need to find uploaded files that group or other users can still modify.
starting_state: An uploads directory contains files from different owners and groups.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reports files writable by group or other.
when_to_use: Use when reviewing upload directories, generated exports, shared cache files, or restored media.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every group-writable upload is wrong without checking the service group model.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Upload files with group-write or other-write permission bits.

shorts.title: Find writable uploads.
shorts.voiceover: Upload directories are noisy. Start by listing files that someone besides the owner can still modify.
shorts.caption: Writable uploads deserve review.

linkedin.question: Do you audit upload files separately from the upload directory itself?
linkedin.cta: Check writable uploads before broad chmod fixes.

youtube.description: Find upload files that are writable by group or other users.

experiments.variant_b: The directory is not the whole upload risk.
